segments.text: Another Sutai is 3.3.26, these 3 persons are found existing in the world, but 3, there
is a person who is not to be followed, not to be served, not to be honored.
Then there is a person who should be followed, served and honored, and then there is
the one who is to be followed, served and honored with worship and reverence.
Now what sort monks is the one who is not to be followed?
In this case a certain person is inferior to oneself in moral conduct, concentration
and wisdom.
Let me see, la samadhi and panya.
Such a one monks is not to be followed, served or honored, except out of consideration,
out of compassion for him.
Now what sort monks is the one who should be followed?
In this case a certain one is like oneself, in moral conduct, concentration and wisdom.
Such a one should be followed, served and honored.
Why?
With this idea, as we are both proficient in moral conduct, our talk will be of morality, and it
will continue to our prophet, and that will be to our comfort.
As we are both proficient in concentration, in wisdom, our talk will be of these subjects,
and it will continue to our prophet, and that will be to our comfort.
Therefore such a person should be followed, served and honored.
And of what sort monks is the one who is to be followed, served and honored with worship
and reverence.
In this case a certain person is superior to oneself, in moral conduct, concentration and
wisdom.
Such a one should first be worshipped and revered, then followed, served and honored.
Why so?
With this idea, in this way I shall complete the sum to all two of,
morality not yet complete.
Or by wisdom I shall supplement it here and there when it is complete.
Or I shall complete the sum to all two of my concentration not yet complete.
Or I shall supplement it by wisdom or insight here and there when it is complete.
Or again I shall complete the sum to all of my wisdom not yet complete.
Or when complete I shall supplement it here and there by insight.
Therefore such a person should be followed, served and honored with worship and reverence.
These monks are the three persons found existing in the world.
Now here the Buddha is talking about three persons and one is inferior to you in
the treating sila, samadhi and panya, and this kind of person is to be avoided.
Unless you have compassion for that person, you want to help that person.
The second type of person who should be followed and whom you should keep company,
is somebody who is of the same standard with you in sila, samadhi and panya.
This type of person you can keep company like spiritual friend friend in the damma.
The third one is one whom you should follow and also reveal that is somebody who is superior
to you in sila, samadhi and panya, because you can teach you to improve, to complete your sila, samadhi and panya.
So that kind of person who can be your teacher, whom you can learn from,
who should follow him and serve him.
So if you want to find a teacher, there's the kind of teacher you ought to look for,
one who is superior to you, and in sila, samadhi and panya.
The next sutra is 3.3.30, monks. There are these three persons found existing in the world.
What three? The top-seater we brain, the scatter brain, and the man of comprehensive brain.
Of what sort monks is the top-seater we brain, here in a certain person frequency
the monastery to hear damma from the lips of the monks. The monk's teaching damma
that is lovely in the beginning, lovely in the middle, lovely in the end, both in spirit and
in letter. They make play in the holy life perfectly fulfilled in all its purity.
But as he sits there, he pays no heed to that top in its beginning, pays no heed to its
middle, pays no heed to its end. Also when he has risen from his seat, he pays no heed there to.
Just as when a pot is turned upside down, the water poured there on, runs off, and does not stay
in the pot. Even so, in this case, a certain person frequency the monastery, but pays no heed
to that top. Also when he rises from his seat, he pays no heed there to. This one is called
the top-seater we brain. And a what sort monks is the scatter brain? In this case, a certain person
frequency the monastery. As he sits, he pays heed to that top in its beginning, his middle,
and its end. But when he has risen up from his seat, he pays no heed there to. Just as when in a man's
lap, diverse kinds of food are piled together, such as sesame, rice, sweet meats, and due to
be fruits. When he rises from his seat, he scatters all abroad through absent-mindedness.
Even so, monks, in this case, a certain person frequency the monastery. But when he has risen
from his seat, he pays no heed there to. This one is called the scatter brain. Now what sort
monks is the man of comprehensive mind? In this case, a certain person frequency the monastery
to hear dhamma from the lips of the monks. They teach him dhamma that is lovely in the beginning,
lovely in the middle, lovely in the end, both in its spirit and its letter. They make play in
the holy life perfectly fulfilled in all its purity. As he sits there, he pays heed to that top
in its beginning, he pays heed to that top in its middle, he pays heed to its ending.
Also, when he rises from his seat, he still bears it in mind. Just as when a pot is set
upright, the water poured therein accumulates and does not run away. Even so, in this case, a person
frequency the monastery and pays heed to that top. Also, when he rises from his seat, he bears
it in mind, in his beginning, in its middle and in its ending. This one monks is called the man
of comprehensive mind. Such monks are the three persons found existing in the world. I think this
tie is quite plain. He can understand the top seat of a brain person. He doesn't pay attention.
His mind is so scattered. When he is sitting there, placing to the top, he cannot concentrate on
the top. Then when he gets up, after he leaves the monastery, also he is forgotten what the top was all
about. The second one, he pays attention. When the top is going on, but when he goes back,
he does not reflect on the top. He is all going in one ear and gone out the other. It is forgotten
about what was taught instead of reflecting on it. The third one is he pays attention to it
when the top is delivered and even after the top goes back, he still reflects it in his mind
and it remembers what was heard. So, he also can examine yourselves and see which category you fall
into. Now 3.4.31 monks, those families where mother and father are worshiped in the home
are reckoned like unto Brahma. Those families where mother and father are worshiped in the home
are ranked with the teachers of all. The worthy of offerings monks are those families where mother and
father are worshiped in the home. Brahma monks is a term for mother and father. Teachers of all
monks is a term for mother and father. worthy of offerings monks is a term for mother and father.
Why so? Because mother and father do much for children, they bring them up,
nourish and introduce them to the world. This is another sulta where the Buddha is teaching us to have
a lot of love and respect for our parents because we owe a lot to them. If we have a lot of respect
and love for our parents then even there there was a happy respect for us.
3.4.34, thus have I heard, on a certain occasion the exalted one was staying here
alive. At Kau Path in Singh Sapa grew, lodging, on the leaves thrown ground. Now Hattaka of
Alawi was wondering there a foot. And as he went along he saw the exalted one in that place,
seated on the ground thrown with leaves. On seeing him he approached and saluting him sat down at one side.
So seated Hattaka of Alawi said this to the exalted one. Praise the exalted one, live happily.
On the Buddha replied, yes my leg, I live happily and one of those who live happily in the world.
And then Hattaka said, but sir, the winter nights are cold, the dark half of the month is
the time of snowfall. Hard is the ground trampled by the hooves of cattle, thin the carpet of
fallen leaves, sparse are the leaves of the tree, cold are the saffron robs and cold the
gale of wind that blows. Then they exalted one said, still my leg, I live happily. Of those who live
happily in the world I am one. Now my leg I will question you about this and you reply as you
think fit. But think you my leg. Suppose a house father or house father son, as a house with
a gable roof, plastered inside and out, but well fitting doors and casements. Therein is a couch,
spread with long, pleased, woolen, rock, a bit spread of white wool, a covenant and broided with
flowers, spread with a costly skin of antelope. Having a canopy overhead and a scarlet cushion
at each end. Here is a lamb burning and four wives to wait upon him with all their charms. Now what
think you my leg? Would he live happily or not? What do you think? And he replied, yes he would sir,
he is one of those who live happily in the world. And the Buddha said, well now my leg, what do you
think? Would there not arise in that house father or house father son, to a man's of body or mind?
There are that aborn of passion, brahga, so that tortured by them he would live unhappy.
And he replied, they would arise. Buddha said, well my leg, as to those torments of body or of
mind, born of passion, tortured by which he would live unhappy. Then passion has been abandoned by
the tatagata, cut off at the root, made like a palm, tree, stump, made unable to become again,
of a nature not to arise again in future. That is why I live happily. Again, would there not
arise, torments of body or of mind, born of hatred, dosa, so that tortured by them he would live unhappy.
He replied, they would arise, the Buddha said, well my leg, as to those torments, that hatred
has been abandoned by the tatagata, that is why I live happily. Again, would there not arise,
torments of body or mind, born of delusion, bhoha, so that tortured by them he would live unhappy.
He would arise, well my leg, as to those torments, etc., etc., that delusion has been abandoned
by the tatagata, that is why I live happily. Ah, so that is the end of the sutta, so here in
the sutta, the Buddha is living in the forest, staying on the ground, sitting on the ground,
sleeping on the ground, and in India they have winters, in tonight's service are so cold,
and yet the Buddha says he's happy because he's gotten rid of the tree causes of unhappiness,
that is, passion, raga, ah, hatred, dosa, and delusion, mhoha, these tree things are sometimes
in the sutta, is mentioned, not raga, dosa and mhoha, but loba, greed, loba, dosa and mhoha,
here, sometimes is mentioned as raga, dosa and mhoha, raga, is passion or lust,
instead of loba is greed, so that's why sometimes some people don't understand,
the thing, why does a person give up his holy life abandoned comforts of the world and become
unanswered, walking around, ball headed, begging for his food, because as it is plain here,
even though a monk is not comfortable, physically, but mentally, he's much more comfortable
than a lot of lay people, so his happiness is within, and the happiness that is within is of a
deeper nature, as a deeper happiness, then the happiness of a lot of lay people, a lot of lay
people, the happiness depends on external things, and it's so fragile, you love somebody,
even that that person says something nasty to you, and you get heartache, just, ah, you picture
yourself to be opposite, ah, you know, you want people to think, you are such and such,
you're a piercest and such, when somebody criticizes you, yourself image seems to be shattered
and you get so unhappy, because external things, if you depend on external things,
for your happiness, yeah, so fragile, you depend on yourself, on your mind, then it is much
much more stable happiness, 3.4.35, the good I said, monks, there are these three
devil messengers, what three? In this connection a certain one lives an immoral life, indeed,
word and thought, so doing when body breaks up after death, he's reborn in the waste, the
wolf who claims, the downfall in hell, then monks, the waters of hell, sees him by both arms,
and bring him before Yama the Lord, saying, sire, this man had no respect for mother and
father, recluses and brahmanas, he showed no respect to the elders of his clan, let your
majesty inflict due punishment on him, then monks, Yama the Lord, examines him, closely
questions him, and addresses him concerning the first devil messengers see, now my good man,
have you never seen the first devil messengers manifest among men, had you replies,
I have not seen him sire, then says King Yama to him, one, my good man, have you never seen
any human being, a woman or a man, 80 or 90 or 100 years of age, broken down, then in words
like the rafter of a roof, crooked, propped up with a cane, and trembling as he goes along,
and ailing creature passes his prime, with broken teeth, grey head or ballless or hairless,
balled with wrinkle-brow and limbs all-bloached and spotted, then the man says, sire, I have seen,
then says Yama, Lord of that to him, my good man, did it never occur to you as a man of intelligence
and fully grown, I too am subject to old age, I have not overpassed old age, come let's
be act nobly indeed word and thought, then the other says, no sire, I could not, I was negligent,
then monks Yama, Lord of that says to him, my good man, it was true negligence that you did not
act nobly indeed word and thought, truthfully they shall do unto you in accordance with your negligence,
that evil action of yours was not done by mother, father, brother, sister, friends, and comrades,
not by kinsman, devers, recluse, and brahmanas, by yourself alone was it done,
it is just you that will experience the fruit thereof, then monks, Yama, Lord of that, having
examined him, closely questioned, and addressed him concerning the first devar messenger,
does likewise concerning the second devar messenger say, my good man, have you never seen the second devar
messenger many faster among men, and he replies, I have never seen him sire, then says Yama, Lord of that
to him, what, my good man have you never seen among human beings, a woman or a man, sick,
afflicted, suffering from a sore disease, lie, warling in his own filth, there's experiment,
by some lifted up by others put to bed, and he says, sire, I have seen,
then king Yama says, then my good man, did it never occur to you as a man of intelligence and fully
grown, I too am subject to disease, I have not overpassed disease, come let me act nobly indeed word and thought,
then says he sire, I could not, I was negligent, then says Yama, Lord of that to him,
my good man, it was true negligence that you did not act nobly indeed word and thought,
merely they shall do unto you according to your negligence, that evil action of yours,
was not done by mother, father, brother, sister, friends and comrades, etc, by yourself alone was
it done, it is just you that will experience the fruit thereof. Paraporn monks Yama, Lord of death,
having examined him closely concerning the second diva messenger, does like wise concerning the third
diva messenger saying, my good man, have you never seen among human beings, a woman or a man,
a cops, one, two or three days dead, swollen, black and blue, and festering,
heavy plies, sire, I have seen, and Yama says, then my good man, did it never occur to you
as a man of intelligence and fully grown, I too am subject to death, I have not overpassed death,
come let me act nobly indeed word and thought, and says he, sire, I could not, I was negligent,
Yama, Lord of death, says to him, my good man, it was true negligence that you did not act
nobly indeed word and thought, where a lead they shall do unto you according to your negligence,
that evil action of yours was not done by mother, father, etc, etc, by yourself alone was
it done, it is just you that will experience the fruit thereof. Ten monks, having examined him closely
concerning the third day of our messenger, Yama, Lord of death, is silent, thereof on monks,
the waters of hell torture him with a five-fold pinion, they drive a hot iron pin through each hand
and foot, and a fifth through the middle of his breath, thereand he suffers grievous,
violent, sharp, and bitter agonies, but he makes not an end, until that evil deed of his has been
worked out. Then the waters of hell lay him down and plain him down with access, thereand he suffers
bitter agonies, to that evil deed of his has been worked out, next they place him with feet up
and head down and plain him with raises, then they bind him to a chariot and drive him up and down,
over a blazing ground, flaming and all the glow, thereand he suffers bitter,
grievous, violent, sharp, and bitter agonies, then they push him up and down a huge burning mountain
of red hot cold, blazing, flaming and all the glow, and he suffers again grievous, violent,
sharp, and bitter agonies, but he makes not an end, until that evil deed of his has been worked out.
Then they take him, feet up and head down and plunge him into a burning, brazen
cauldron, blazing, flaming and all the glow, there he is cooked and rises to the surface with
the scum, so doing once he comes up and once he goes down and once he goes across, thereand he
suffers agonies, grievous, violent, sharp, and bitter, yet he makes not an end until that evil deed of
his has been worked out. Thereupon monks, the waters of hell toss him into the great hell.
Now, now monks, this great hell is described thus.
Four square of the great hell standed with four gates divided and partitioned with the wall of
iron, good, of iron is the roof, its floor of iron too, dazzling and hot and flashing all around
a hundred leeks, stands fast forever immovable. Once upon a time monks, Yama, Lord of Death, thought
thus to himself. True it is I think that they who in the world do evil deeds in diverse ways,
thus suffer attribution. Oh, that, oh, if only I could win both as a human being,
if only a tatagata were born into the world and other hand who is a fully enlightened one.
Only I might sit at the feet of that exalted one and then that exalted one would teach me Dhamma,
and then I might learn Dhamma from the exalted one. Now, monks, I see this not hearing it from
some recluse or prominence, lay, but what I myself had known and seen and heard that do I declare
unto you? So this is also quite interesting Sutra where we talk about King Yama, Yen Loh Wang,
Yam Loh Wang, it appears from the Sutra that there are many hells, hells is like a
region, you know, and each region of hell is great hell. There is a King, and that King is
called King Yama, so of the various hells, they are various King Yama. And the King Yama is
addressed as Dhamma by the hell being, you know, because he is in a much better condition than the
other hell beings, so they call him Dhamma. So when the, when the mentioned here the three Dhamma
messages, he might either be the tree heavenly messages or the tree messages from hell,
and you can see from here the type of suffering that a person undergoes in hell, there are some
other sutras, which also mentioned something like this, but even worse, where they suffer from
one hell, the suffer all kinds of approaches, and then they are like a fugitive, you know, trying to run
away because when they are caught and they are caught by these beings in hell. So they are running,
trying to avoid these beings in hell, running, running, and once in a long while, the King
eats of that hell will open. Once in a long while, and when it opens, I know only opens for a short
time, and they rush out, and some of them manage to rush out, some of them are unable to rush out.
And after they rush out, they think they have got out of hell, but what happens is that they
fall into another hell, another different kind of hell, and they suffer again, again, like a fugitive,
running, running, and being tortured, and all that. And once in a blue moon, again, the gates of
that hell open, and some of them rush out again, and then they fall into another hell,
by being they fall into a hell, which is like a river, and that river, and it's like I sit,
they are burned in that river. And then they keep going from one hell to another, and then,
finally they come back to the hell where they started. So you see, the undergoes, physical,
torture as well as mental torture. So you can imagine if any kind of you can't find any
worse suffering than to be in that hell. And the great hell here is described as being of
the iron roof, the iron floor, and so hot, and you can't run out of that place. It's like
the oven. And this king Yamaha, even here, the Buddha says, makes a wish that one day he can be
reborn as a human being, so that he can hear the Dhamma, the Buddha's Dhamma, and so that he can
get out of Samsara. So now we are at this point where we are able to hear the Dhamma. Maybe previously,
the Buddha said that most of us are practically all of us have been through Samsara for extremely
long time, and most of the rebirths we have gone through. Maybe at one time we were king Yamaha,
also. Maybe at one time we also wish that one day we get birth as a human being and we might
hear the Dhamma. So now we are at this stage where we are able to hear the Dhamma. We should not
let the chains go up lightly. We should take the trouble to really understand the Dhamma and
practice so that we can get out of hell and never fall into this woeful plains again,
probably many times you have already fallen into the woeful plains. I think some of us now you can
see that in our dreams, we have been through certain places like hell. So in the fact that you can
come up in our dreams maybe, this is from our memory in our past life, and we have been in such places.
3.4.36 months on the 8th day of the lunar half-month, the ministers who are counselors of the
four great kings, who are circumambulated this woe, to see whether many four among men,
pay reverence to mother and father, to recluse and prominence, and show reverence to the
elders of the clan, observe the sabbath or Upusata, keep the video and do good works.
On the 14th day of the lunar half-month and the four sons of the four great kings,
to accumulate this woe, to see whether many four pay reverence to mother and father, to recluse
and prominence, and show reverence to the elders of the clan, observe the sabbath, keep the
video and do good works. On the 15th day, which is a sabbath, the four great kings in person,
so commemorate this woe, to see whether many four pay reverence to mother and father, to recluse
and prominence, and show reverence to the elders of the clan, observe the sabbath,
keep the video and do good works. Now, monks, if few, be among those men who do these things,
the four great kings report the matter to the devas of the 33, Tabatim Sahebanna,
as they sit in conclave in the hall of righteousness, saying,
my good lords, few among men are they who pay reverence to mother and father, etc.
and do good works. Then monks, the devas of the 33 are displeased, and say,
surely says, the deva hosts will diminish and the asura hosts will increase.
But monks, if many are they among men who pay reverence to mother and father to recluse and
prominence, who show reverence to the elders of the clan, observe the sabbath,
keep the video and do good works. Then the four great kings report the matter to the devas
of the 33, saying, good, my lords. Many are they among men who pay reverence to mother and father,
etc. Therein monks, the devas of the 33 are glad, and say, surely says,
the deva hosts will increase and the asura hosts will decrease.
This and other are quite interesting, so time, which tells us that there are devas who
go around the world and observe us, and we are not aware of it. Just like when we observe ants,
the ants are so small, we look from the top, we see what they are doing, but they don't know.
They can't see us then, because the feel of vision is so limited, so near, to in the same way,
our feel of vision, now what we can see is so limited, unless we develop psychic power.
So the devas might be looking at us, and we look at the describe that we can't see them.
When they talk about the lunar half-month, it's because in India, the calendar is not
monthly in like 30 days, like us, it is fortnightly, fortnightly, and the fortnight can either be 14 days,
or can be 15 days. So on the 8th day, we get an issue, it's a small part, the 8th day of the lunar
month, 15, then the 23rd day, and the 30th day, or the 29th day, that is out there about that
tradition, is the first the 8th, the 15, not sorry, not the first, the 8th, you know, the 8th,
the 8th, then the 15th, the 23rd, and the 30th, or 29th. Now this last day, 29th or 30th,
for the Mayana, there's made it a bit different, they made it the first first of the lunar month.
That's why in our television, we either recite the precepts on the partmoca, on the 15th,
lunar day, or the 29th or 30th, rest in Chinese, they always talk about joy,
in television, we don't talk about joy, we start with your part, the 8th day.
